elements.sent-2411-001: E-commerce app users exhibit behaviors that are inherently logically consistent.
elements.sent-2411-002: A series of multi-scenario user behaviors interconnect to form the scene-level all-domain user moveline, which ultimately reveals the user's true intention.
elements.sent-2411-003: Traditional Click-Through Rate (CTR) prediction methods typically focus on the item-level interaction between the target item\footnote{Target Item refers to the candidate item to be scored in a standard CTR prediction task, typically serving as the sole primary key in a given sample.}
elements.sent-2411-004: However, the scene-level interaction between the target item and the user moveline remains underexplored.
elements.sent-2411-005: There are two challenges when modeling the interaction with preceding all-domain user moveline: (i) Heterogeneity between items and scenes: Unlike traditional user behavior sequences that utilize items as carriers, the user moveline utilizes scenes as carriers.
elements.sent-2411-006: The heterogeneity between items and scenes complicates the process of aligning interactions within a unified representation space.
elements.sent-2411-007: However, it is impossible to establish a complete temporal alignment that clearly identifies which specific scene-level behaviors correspond to which item-level behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-008: To address these challenges and pioneer modeling user intent from the perspective of the all-domain moveline, we propose a general CTR prediction model, dubbed All-domain Moveline Evolution Network (AMEN).
elements.sent-2411-009: AMEN not only transfers interactions between items and scenes to homogeneous representation spaces, but also introduces a Temporal Sequential Pairwise (TSP) mechanism to understand the nuanced associations between scene-level and item-level behaviors, ensuring that the all-domain user moveline differentially influences CTR predictions for user's favored and unfavored items.
elements.sent-2411-010: Extensive online A/B testing on large-scale real-world data demonstrates that our method achieves state-of-the-art online performance, yielding a +11.6\% increase in post-view click-through-conversion rate (CTCVR).
elements.sent-2411-011: In e-commerce recommendation systems, the Click-Through Rate (CTR) prediction task involves estimating the CTR score of a target item for a specific user based on their historical behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-012: Traditional approaches for modeling user historical behavior concentrate on the item level, employing methods such as DIN~\cite{DIN , DIEN~\cite{DIEN , DSIN~\cite{DSIN , SIM~\cite{SIM , and DIAN~\cite{DIAN .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: These methods typically construct a sequence of user's interacted items by extracting explicit feedback like clicks, add-to-carts, favorites, and purchases.
elements.sent-2411-014: The relevance between the target item and the user's historical item-level behavior sequence is then assessed through Multi-head Target Attention (MHTA)~\cite{DIN , thereby facilitating the CTR prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-015: In contrast to item-level approaches, a more comprehensive approach considers a series of user behaviors across multiple scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-016: These include activities like browsing item feeds on the homepage, searching for topics, collecting coupons, and visiting brand stores, etc., collectively forming what we refer to as the scene-level all-domain user moveline.
elements.sent-2411-017: This moveline offers a broader perspective on user behavior, ultimately revealing the user's true intentions, such as browsing, purchasing, or seeking discounts.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.92\linewidth]{graphs/example.png} \caption{A typical showcase of how the preceding scene-level all-domain user moveline uncovers user's true intention. A user visits the app's marketing channel twice at time $t_0$ and $t_1$, with preceding user moveline $\mathcal{\vec{M}}_{t_0}$ and $\mathcal{\vec{M}}_{t_1}$, respectively.} \label{fig:example}
elements.sent-2411-018: To demonstrate the motivation intuitively, we illustrate a typical showcase in Figure~\ref{fig:example .}
elements.sent-2411-019: At time $t_0$, A user enters the app's marketing channel, with the preceding user moveline denoted as $\mathcal{\vec{M _{t_0}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: The CTR model recommends a blue cup $e_{t_0}$ to the user, but the user does not click on it.
elements.sent-2411-021: After leaving the channel, the user searches for Bicycle and collects a coupon for the Sport \& Outdoor category, leading to the updated user moveline $\mathcal{\vec{M _{t_1}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: When the user returns to the marketing channel at time $t_1$ with the preceding user moveline $\mathcal{\vec{M _{t_1}$, the CTR model recommends a "bicycle" $e_{t_1}$ to the user, which the user clicks on, generating once positive feedback.. Motivation.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Apparently, the user's actions between $t_0$ and $t_1$ offer crucial insights into their real-time intent, which cannot be fully captured if we focus solely on item interactions.
elements.sent-2411-024: Thus, the click at $t_1$ cannot be directly associated with the non-item-level behaviors from the yellow-highlighted segment in Figure~\ref{fig:example of moveline $\mathcal{\vec{M _{t_1}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Unlike conventional user moveline modeling approaches that often rely on manually defined strategies based on enumerated conditions, our method involves user moveline interactions into the CTR prediction ranking stage, addressing previously overlooked scene-level behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-026: By leveraging the preceding user moveline at each moment, we calculate a moveline reward and add it as a bias term in the final CTR prediction score, enabling dynamic adjustments based on the user's true intent.
elements.sent-2411-027: We propose All-domain Moveline Evolution Network (AMEN), a novel and robust CTR prediction model that redefines the understanding of user intent from an all-domain moveline perspective.
elements.sent-2411-028: We identify two critical characteristics of the all-domain user moveline and present corresponding foundational methodologies in AMEN:.
elements.sent-2411-029: Heterogeneity Between Items and Scenes: Unlike traditional user behavior sequences that utilize items as the primary focus, the all-domain user moveline adopts scenes as its basis, thereby incorporating non-item interactions.
elements.sent-2411-030: However, the heterogeneity between items and scenes presents a challenge for aligning interactions within a uniform representation space.
elements.sent-2411-031: To address this, we explore two pathways: item-to-scene and scene-to-item, transferring the interactions between items and scenes into homogeneous representation spaces.
elements.sent-2411-032: For the item-to-scene pathway, we construct the Active Intention Sequence (AISeq) using items exposed in the user's search result scenes and shop visit scenes within the moveline.
elements.sent-2411-033: Subsequently, we compute the item-level MHTA between the target item and the AISeq to incorporate the item-level user moveline interaction.
elements.sent-2411-034: For the scene-to-item pathway, we employ a prompting-like methodology to create a virtual scene-level target prompt, using the target item as the primary key along with contextual side information from the current recommendation scenario.
elements.sent-2411-035: This prompt is homogeneous with other nodes in the user moveline.
elements.sent-2411-036: Then we calculate the scene-level MHTA between the prompt and the user moveline to incorporate scene-level user moveline interaction.
elements.sent-2411-037: Temporal Misalignment of Linked Scene-Level and Item-Level Behaviors: In the preceding user moveline with a fixed sampling length, certain critical scene-level behaviors are closely linked to subsequent item-level behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-038: To capture the critical temporal dynamics and precisely assess the influence of moveline state changes, we introduce the Temporal Sequential Pairwise (TSP) mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-039: This approach differs from traditional pairwise learning strategies~\cite{pairwise1,pairwise2,listwise2 , which typically establish contrasts by building pairs of target item and other items within the same exposure page context.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Instead, TSP builds pairs by target item and diff items with different feedback labels, gathered from the user’s exposure page context at various time points.
elements.sent-2411-041: Through an auxiliary TSP task during offline training, we ensure that the all-domain user moveline distinctly impacts CTR predictions for favored and unfavored items.
elements.sent-2411-042: To summarize, the main contributions of this work are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-043: We align the interactions between items and scenes within unified representation spaces and propose the Temporal Sequential Pairwise (TSP) mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-044: TSP ensures that the preceding all-domain user moveline distinctly impacts CTR predictions for favored and unfavored items.
elements.sent-2411-045: We conduct extensive experiments to validate the effectiveness of AMEN.
elements.sent-2411-046: Online A/B testing demonstrates that AMEN achieves a new state-of-the-art performance in Taobao marketing channel, boosting post-view click-through-conversion rate (CTCVR) by 11.6\%.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.92\linewidth]{graphs/moxing.png} \caption{The architecture of All-domain Moveline Evolution Network (AMEN). The CTR prediction module serves as the online inference architecture, which merges the outputs of item-to-scene Scene-level Attention and scene-to-item Item-level Attention. The TSP mechanism in the Scene-level Attention ensures that the preceding all-domain user moveline distinctly impacts user's favored and unfavored items.} \label{AMEN}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{graphs/prompting.png} \caption{Item-to-Scene in the inference feed-forward stage, generating the moveline reward $r$.} \label{fig:prompting}
elements.sent-2411-047: The proposed All-domain Moveline Evolution Network (AMEN) comprises the CTR prediction module and the Temporal Sequential Pairwise (TSP) mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-048: The CTR prediction module serves as online inference architecture, aligning interactions between items and scenes into homogeneous representation spaces by dedicated scene-to-item and item-to-scene pathways.
elements.sent-2411-049: The TSP mechanism involves an auxiliary task during offline training, ensuring that the preceding all-domain user moveline distinctly impacts user's favored and unfavored items.
elements.sent-2411-050: This is achieved by contrasting pairs that consist of the target item and diff items with different feedback labels, which are collected from the user's exposure page context at various time points.
elements.sent-2411-051: We construct the Active Intention Sequence (AISeq) by items exposed in the user's search result scenes and shop visit scenes within the moveline.
elements.sent-2411-052: Denoting the AISeq as $E_{ais =[e_{a1 ,e_{a2 ,$ $…,e_{ak ]$, the target item as $e_t$, the item-level MHTA~\cite{DIN is defined as follows: \begin{equation}\label{mhta12} \textbf{H}_{ais} = \mathrm{MHTA}(e_t, \textbf{E}_{ais}) = \mathrm{concat}(head_{1}, head_{2},\ldots, head_{h}),\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{mhta11} head_{i}=\textbf{W}_{i}^{V}\textbf{E}_{ais} \cdot Softmax(\textbf{W}_{i}^{Q}e_t\,(\textbf{W}_{i}^{K}\textbf{E}_{ais})^{\top})/\sqrt{d},\end{equation} where $d$ is the dimension of hidden space, $h$ is the number of attention heads, $W_{i ^{Q ,W_{i ^{K ,W_{i ^{V \in \mathbb{R ^{d \times d/h}$ are learnable parameters.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: The MHTA of AISeq is concatenated with the user profile $E_{user}$, the target item $e_t$, the MHTA between the target item and user's short-term behavior sequence $H_{short}$, the MHTA between the target item and user's long-term behavior sequence $H_{long}$.
elements.sent-2411-054: This concatenated term is then fed into a feed-forward neural network called Main Net: \begin{equation}\label{mainnet} \hat{y}_{main}= \mathrm{MLP}(\mathrm{concat}(\textbf{H}_{ais} , \textbf{H}_{short} , \textbf{H}_{long} , E_{user} , e_{t}))\end{equation} Item-to-Scene.
elements.sent-2411-055: We employ a prompting-like methodology to create a virtual scene-level target prompt, using the target item $e_t$ as the primary key along contextual side information $E_{scene}$ from the current recommendation scenario: \begin{equation}m_{t} = \mathrm{Prompting}(E_{scene}\rightarrow e_{t}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-056: The scene-level target prompt is already aligned with other nodes in the preceding moveline in representation.
elements.sent-2411-057: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:prompting , during the inference feed-forward stage, only the scene-level MHTA corresponding to the target prompt is calculated: \begin{equation}\textbf{G}=\mathrm{MHTA}(m_{t},\mathcal{\vec{M}}).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-058: The MHTA $G$ is processed through a multi-layer Reward Net, which generates the moveline reward for the current target item: \begin{equation}r = Rwd(\textbf{G}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-059: The movement reward is subsequently added to the logits output of the Main Net.
elements.sent-2411-060: With $\sigma$ denoting the Sigmoid function, the final inference output is expressed as: \begin{equation}\hat{y} = \sigma (\hat{y}_{main} + r).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-061: We aim to not only calibrate CTR scores with the entire user moveline, but also to sensitively capture nuanced state changes in the user moveline that greatly influence user intent.
elements.sent-2411-062: In traditional pairwise approaches, the contrastive pairs are matched within the same exposure page context, and the final CTR scores of these pairs are contrasted.
elements.sent-2411-063: In contrast, our TSP matches contrastive pairs by the target item and another diff item that carries different feedback label.
elements.sent-2411-064: These diff items are gathered from the user’s exposure page context at various time points.
elements.sent-2411-065: TSP solely contrasts the moveline rewards, ensuring the prominence and independence of the user moveline branch.. Formulation.
elements.sent-2411-066: As shown in Figure~\ref{AMEN , for a target item $e_{t_0}$, we obtain a diff item $e_{t_1}$ that has an inconsistent click label to build the contrastive pair.}
elements.sent-2411-067: We calculate the moveline reward $r$ for the target item $e_{t_0}$ by the moveline $\mathcal{\vec{M _{t_0}$, and $r'$ for the diff item $e_{t_1}$ by the moveline $\mathcal{\vec{M _{t_1}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Then we amplify the gap between $r$ and $r'$.
elements.sent-2411-069: The overall optimization objective can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\underset{W,W_{Rwd}}{\arg\min} \quad [\,|y-(\hat{y} + r)|_1, \,\,\left | r' - r \right |_{1}\,],\end{equation} where $y$ denotes the groundtruth click label for the target item, $W$ denotes all the model parameters, and $W_{Rwd}$ denotes all parameters relevant for calculating the moveline reward.
elements.sent-2411-070: For the first term in the optimization objective, we adapt the cross-entropy loss function for pointwise~\cite{pointwise1,pointwise2,DIN CTR prediction on dataset $\mathcal{D}$: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{CE}= -\frac{1}{|\mathcal{D}|}\sum_{ \mathcal{D}}{ ( y\log(\hat{y})+(1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})) },\end{equation} For the second term in the optimization objective, we adapt BPR loss function~\cite{bpr which serves as an auxiliary loss for pairwise contrast: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{bpr}} = -\frac{1}{|\mathcal{D}|}\sum_{\mathcal{D}}{\log{\sigma(\mathbb{I}(y')(r' - r))}},\end{equation} \begin{equation}\mathbb{I}(y') = \begin{cases} 1 \text{if } y' = 1, -1 \text{if } y' = 0, \end{cases}\end{equation} where $y'$ denotes the groundtruth click label for the diff item, $\mathbb{I (y')$ denotes the direction alignment function, which ensures that the optimization process consistently aims to enhance the moveline reward of user's favored items in comparison to those unfavored.}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: The final loss during offline training is: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L} = w_1 \cdot \mathcal{L}_{CE} + w_2 \cdot \mathcal{L}_{bpr},\end{equation} where $w_1$ and $w_2$ are hyperparameters to balance loss weights.
elements.sent-2411-072: It is important to note that the TSP mechanism is applicable only during offline training, due to the distinction between offline and online computational graphs.
elements.sent-2411-073: During online inference, only the real-time preceding user moveline is required as input to calculate the moveline reward for the target item, while the branch corresponding to the diff item is truncated.
elements.sent-2411-075: Initially, we consider collecting diff items from the global sample space of the Taobao app.
elements.sent-2411-076: However, both online and offline experiments yield poor performance.
elements.sent-2411-077: We speculate that the inconsistency in scoring scenarios for paired items may be the most significant factor, as controlling variables is essential.
elements.sent-2411-078: For instance, when a target item displayed in the search scenario is paired with a diff item displayed in the marketing channel scenario, the effect of these varying contexts on user behavior tends to overshadow the influence of different preceding user movelines.
elements.sent-2411-079: Since our experiments are primarily conducted on the marketing channel of the Taobao app, we constrain the sampling space exclusively to the marketing channel domain, resulting in a return-to-normal online and offline performance.
elements.sent-2411-080: To effectively capture more significant changes in the user moveline, we restrict the time interval between the target item and the sampled diff item to between 60 seconds and 7 days.
elements.sent-2411-081: If both items originate from the same session, the preceding user movements may not differ significantly, or even are totally the same.
elements.sent-2411-082: This limits the effectiveness of the TSP contrast mechanism.. Effective Match Coverage.
elements.sent-2411-083: Following the constraint of the sampling space of diff items from the global domain to the marketing channel domain, the effective match coverage rate decreased from 64\% to 32\%.
elements.sent-2411-084: During offline training, in order to maintain the stability of the BPR loss magnitude, we only include successfully matched samples in the BPR loss calculation, and the loss is normalized based on the number of successfully matched samples within the batch.
elements.sent-2411-085: We conduct extensive experiments on an industrial dataset gathered from the Taobao marketing channel.
elements.sent-2411-086: The features and click labels are accurately restored according to the traffic logs.
elements.sent-2411-087: The training set includes data from January 2024 to June 2024, and the test set includes data from the Taobao 618 Shopping Carnival in June 2024, which is the second largest explosive promotional event of the year.
elements.sent-2411-088: Detailed information about the datasets is presented in Table \ref{tab:dataset .}
elements.figure-2411-004: \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{-0.5pt} \setlength{\belowcaptionskip}{-0.2cm} \caption{ Statistics of the industrial dataset} \begin{tabular}{llll} Dataset \#Users \#Items \#Instances Train 180.7M 21.9M 6.7B Test 23.2M 7.7M 0.6B \end{tabular} \label{tab:dataset}
elements.sent-2411-089: Both AMEN and its competitors are developed using a unified backbone and feature format.
elements.sent-2411-090: We utilize the AdaGrad optimizer, starting with an initial learning rate of 0.001, which decays over time as training progresses.
elements.sent-2411-092: The number of heads $h$ in MHTA is 4.
elements.sent-2411-093: The hyper-parameters $w_1$, $w_2$ are set to 1.0, 0.1, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-094: All experiments are implemented on a distributed TensorFlow-based framework, using tide scheduling CPUs.. Competitors.
elements.sent-2411-095: We compare three well-known CTR prediction models, Wide\&Deep~\cite{WideDeep , DIN~\cite{DIN , and SIM~\cite{SIM , along with Domain Bias Adaptive Network (DBA).}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: As a variant of SIM, DBA demonstrates exceptional generalization in the Taobao marketing channel, via introducing domain-biased self-adaptation mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-097: DBA is continuously trained on nearly 100 billion samples, positioning DBA as the current optimal baseline in the experimental scenario.
elements.sent-2411-098: We employ AUC and GAUC~\cite{DIN as our evaluation metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-099: AUC quantifies the likelihood that a positive sample receives a higher score than a negative sample.
elements.sent-2411-100: GAUC is the weighted average of AUC, accounting for varying user activity levels, which helps to mitigate the effects of user activity bias.
elements.sent-2411-101: The experimental results on the industrial dataset are presented in Table~\ref{tab:auc and Table~\ref{tab:auc2 .}}
elements.figure-2411-005: \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{-0.5pt} \setlength{\belowcaptionskip}{-0.2cm} \caption{ Comparative experiments with famous methods and baseline} \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2} \begin{tabular}{{lll}} Model AUC GAUC Wide\&Deep 0.7216 0.6584 DIN 0.7410 0.6595 SIM 0.7458 0.6657 DBA 0.7621 0.6851 AMEN 0.7694 0.6867 \end{tabular} \label{tab:auc}
elements.figure-2411-006: \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{-0.5pt} \setlength{\belowcaptionskip}{-0.2cm} \caption{ Ablation experiments of inner methods} \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2} \begin{tabular}{{lllllll}} Model AISeq TSP DifGS $w_{2}$ AUC GAUC DBA 0.7621 0.6851 AMEN 0.1 0.7660 0.6820 AMEN \checkmark 0.1 0.7687 0.6856 AMEN \checkmark \checkmark \checkmark 0.1 0.7531 0.6720 AMEN \checkmark \checkmark 0.5 0.7630 0.6786 AMEN \checkmark \checkmark 0.02 0.7625 0.6854 AMEN \checkmark \checkmark 0.1 0.7694 0.6867 \end{tabular} \begin{tablenotes} \item* AISeq is the Active Intention Sequence. TSP is the Temporal Sequential Pairwise mechanism. DifGS means sampling diff items from global space. $w_2$ is the fusion weight hyperparameter of the BPR loss $\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{bpr}}$. \end{tablenotes} \label{tab:auc2}
elements.sent-2411-102: We prove the effectiveness of each module through ablation experiments, as shown in Table \ref{tab:auc .}
elements.sent-2411-103: First, we compare DBA with SIM, a widely used model in industry.
elements.sent-2411-104: Our experiments indicate that DBA significantly outperforms SIM, highlighting the advanced capability of DBA as a baseline model.
elements.sent-2411-105: Compared to DBA (Baseline), the item-to-scene scene-level MHTA lifts AUC +0.39pt\footnote{pt refers to percentage point.. but GAUC -0.31pt.}
elements.sent-2411-107: The scene-to-item AISeq MHTA lifts AUC +0.07pt and GAUC +0.11pt.
elements.sent-2411-108: Sampling diff items from the global space (DifGS) lifts AUC -0.63pt and GAUC -1.47pt, illustrating the necessity to constrain the sampling space for diff items.
elements.sent-2411-109: We also validate the performance difference with respect to the hyperparameter $w_2$ set at 0.02, 0.1, and 0.5, finding 0.1 to yield the best results in our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-110: Briefly, our method AMEN advances AUC +0.73pt and GAUC +0.16pt compared to the baseline DBA.
elements.sent-2411-111: To further investigate the impact of the TSP on moveline rewards, we record the moveline reward results during offline inference for the test set.
elements.sent-2411-112: As illustrated in Figure~\ref{gailvmidu , we plot the probability density distribution fitting graph, which is structured along two dimensions: the introduction of TSP (TSP vs. Non.TSP) and the groundtruth label of the sample (click vs. unclick).}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{graphs/gailvmidu.png} \caption{Probability density distribution of the moveline reward.} \label{gailvmidu}
elements.sent-2411-113: To fairly evaluate the relative levels and density/sparsity of moveline reward across different settings, we normalize the moveline reward values and categorize them into 100 buckets.
elements.sent-2411-114: The x-axis represents the normalized buckets of moveline reward, where larger buckets indicate higher moveline rewards.
elements.sent-2411-115: The y-axis represents the proportion of moveline rewards that fall into each bucket.
elements.sent-2411-116: The following conclusions can be drawn: (i) Regardless of whether the TSP is adopted, the moveline reward distribution curve for clicks is generally shifted to the right compared to that for unclicks.
elements.sent-2411-117: This indicates that the model has learned to assign higher moveline rewards to samples labeled as clicks.
elements.sent-2411-118: This implies that TSP enhances the model's ability to distinguish user behaviors effectively.
elements.sent-2411-119: We apply online A/B testing during Taobao 618 Shopping Carnival, the second largest explosive promotional event on the Taobao app each year.
elements.sent-2411-120: As shown in Table \ref{tab:abtest , our method improves both click and transaction indicators across two main scenarios in the Taobao marketing channel.}
elements.sent-2411-121: Here we present the results for purchase-related indicators, as enhancing CTR can often adversely impact conversion rate (CVR)~\cite{cvr1 .}
elements.sent-2411-122: This means that click indicators and transaction indicators can be interconverted, with their respective weights adjusted according to specific business strategies.
elements.sent-2411-123: To quantify the true improvement in online performance, we utilize the post-view click-through-conversion rate (CTCVR)~\cite{esmm as the metric, which encompasses both click and transaction indicators.}
elements.sent-2411-124: Our method effectively overcomes this multi-objective balancing seesaw effect, leading to a net improvement in overall performance.
elements.figure-2411-008: \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{-0.5pt} \setlength{\belowcaptionskip}{-0.2cm} \caption{ Online A/B testing results} 0.8\linewidth{!}{ { \begin{tabular}{{lllll}} Scenario CTCVR CTR CVR GMV Feeds +11.6\% Stable +7.4\% Stable Floors +4.2\% +20.6\% +2.0\% +20.1\% \end{tabular} } } \begin{tablenotes} \item* The value in table indicates the Lift Rate compared to the baseline DBA. CTCVR refers to the scenario-guided purchase probability of a daily user view. CTR here refers to the scenario-guided click probability of a daily user view. CVR here refers to the scenario-guided purchase probability of a daily user click. GMV here refers to the scenario-guided total transaction amount. \end{tablenotes} \label{tab:abtest}
elements.sent-2411-125: This paper demonstrates All-domain Moveline Evolution Network (AMEN), a general CTR prediction model that redefines the understanding of user intent from an all-domain moveline perspective.
elements.sent-2411-126: AMEN aligns the interaction between items and user movelines into homogeneous representation spaces, and proposes Temporal Sequential Pairwise (TSP) mechanism to ensure that the preceding all-domain user moveline distinctly impacts user’s favored and unfavored items.
elements.sent-2411-127: Experiments show that AMEN sets a new state-of-the-art performance on Taobao, boosting visit conversion rate by 11.6\%.. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format \balance.}
